result:
The old woman at the corner table orders the same thing every morning: black coffee, no sugar, and silence. I've been serving her for three months now, and we've never exchanged more than seven words. "The usual?" I ask. "Please," she responds. Sometimes "thank you" when I set the cup down. The ceramic clinks against the saucer like a tiny bell.

Today she's wearing purple gloves, which strikes me as odd because it's July and the air conditioning in here barely works. They're velvet, I think, or something that wants to be velvet. She turns the pages of her newspaper with them on, leaving small smudges on the edges. I wonder if the ink stains the fabric.

The coffee shop is mostly empty at 6 AM. Just her, me, and Marcus who's pretending to write a screenplay on his laptop but is actually scrolling through his ex-girlfriend's Instagram. I know because I can see the reflection in the window behind him. Her new boyfriend has a boat. Marcus doesn't have a boat. This seems to matter immensely to him.

The espresso machine hisses. I pull shots for no one, just to keep my hands busy. The movements are automatic now—tamp, twist, pull, steam. My body knows this dance better than any other. Sometimes I catch myself making these motions in my sleep, my girlfriend tells me. Phantom lattes.

The woman in purple gloves stands up. This is unusual. She normally stays until seven-fifteen, precise as clockwork. But it's only six-thirty. She walks to the counter, and I notice she's limping slightly. New hip, maybe. My grandmother walked like that after hers.

"I want to tell you something," she says.

My hand hovers over the register, uncertain. "Okay."

"Forty-three years ago, I killed a man in this building."

The milk steamer chooses this moment to screech. Marcus looks up from his phone. The woman doesn't flinch.

"It was self-defense," she continues, as if discussing the weather. "This wasn't a coffee shop then. It was a print shop. My husband's print shop. He was drunk again, angrier than usual. Had a wrench in his hand."

I don't know what to say, so I say nothing. This seems right somehow.

"I pushed him. He fell backwards, hit his head on the corner of the printing press. Right about—" she points to where we keep the bag of coffee beans "—there."

Marcus is definitely listening now, laptop forgotten.

"Nobody asked questions. He was known for drinking, for falling. The police came, wrote it down as an accident. Case closed. I sold the shop, moved away for thirty years. Then I came back, and it was this." She gestures around at the exposed brick, the Edison bulbs, the chalkboard menu.

"Why are you telling me?" I ask.

She pulls off one purple glove, then the other. Her hands are spotted with age, but steady. "Because you're the only person in this town who doesn't try to fill my silence with noise. Everyone else wants to chat, to know my story, to be friendly. You just let me be. That's worth something."

She places a fifty-dollar bill on the counter. "For the coffee. All of it. These three months."

"That's too much—"

"It's not enough." She turns to leave, then stops. "Do you believe in ghosts?"

"I don't know."

"Good answer. I don't either. But sometimes, early in the morning, I hear the printing press. That's why I come here. To listen. To make sure it's really gone."

She walks out, leaving the purple gloves on the counter. I pick them up. They're softer than I expected, and warm. There's a label inside: "Property of St. Mary's Hospital." I Google it later. St. Mary's closed in 1981.

Marcus comes to the counter after she leaves. "That was intense," he says.

"Yeah."

"You think it's true?"

I look at the spot she pointed to. There's a dark stain on the floor I've never noticed before. Could be coffee. Could be anything. "Does it matter?"

He considers this, then returns to his laptop. To the boats and the ex-girlfriend and the life he's not living.

I keep the gloves under the counter. She doesn't come back the next day, or the next. On the third day, I check the obituaries. Nothing. On the fourth day, I stop checking.

Two weeks later, a young woman comes in at 6 AM. Orders black coffee, no sugar. Sits at the corner table. Opens a book. Doesn't try to make conversation. I bring her the coffee and she nods, almost imperceptibly.

The gloves are still under the counter. Sometimes I touch them, just to make sure they're real. They always are. Soft and purple and impossible, like the best secrets.

The espresso machine hisses. Marcus types nothing on his laptop. The morning slides forward into afternoon, and I think about ghosts, about the sounds old buildings make, about the stories we tell strangers when we can't bear to tell them to ourselves.

Somewhere, maybe, an old woman is listening to a printing press that doesn't exist. Or maybe she never existed either. Maybe I invented her out of the need for mystery, for something beyond the daily grind of coffee grounds.

But the gloves are real. I'm holding them now. They smell like lavender and time.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
